summary: Julia Evans explains the bias-variance decomposition, a mathematical framework that clarifies why machine learning techniques like ensembles and regularization actually work. Coming from a pure math background, she was frustrated by machine learning's reliance on heuristics rather than theory. The bias-variance decomposition theorem shows that prediction error decomposes into bias squared plus variance plus irreducible error, which provides actionable insight for model building. She carefully labels which parts of her explanation are rigorous math versus practical heuristics. The post demonstrates how ensembles reduce variance by averaging correlated models, and how regularization trades increased bias for decreased variance. Throughout, she maintains intellectual honesty about where the math ends and the hand-waving begins.
key_points: The bias-variance decomposition theorem proves that prediction error equals bias squared plus variance plus irreducible error, giving a mathematical foundation for understanding model performance
Ensembles work because averaging multiple high-variance, low-bias models reduces variance while preserving the same bias, with variance reduction depending on correlation between models
The bias-variance 'tradeoff' is actually a heuristic observation, not a mathematical necessity — there's no proven reason you must trade one for the other
Regularization increases bias but lowers variance, and can win overall if the variance reduction exceeds the bias increase, as demonstrated by the James-Stein estimator
Most practical claims in machine learning (like 'complex models have lower bias') lack mathematical backing and are empirical observations that happen to work
money_quotes: Everything was 'well it works in practice' and so little of it was math. I was a pure math major at the time, so arguments like 'well it works in practice' made me REALLY MAD.
I've annotated each section by whether it's math or whether it a 'works in practice' heuristic (aka bullshit, but useful bullshit).
This is a theorem that you can find in An Introduction to Statistical Learning. This is math so it will never let us down.
There's no math reason I know of you have to have a tradeoff between bias and variance.
Not knowing how machine learning methods work means I can't debug them, and that means I can't be amazing. which obviously isn't acceptable.
The James-Stein estimator is a way to estimate the mean that gives you lower error. It's a biased estimator, but its error is lower. This is amazing.

Understanding why ensembles work is awesome – it means that if an ensemble method isn't helping you, you can understand why! It's probably because the correlation between the models in your ensemble is too high.
key_insight: The bias-variance decomposition is one of the few pieces of actual math in machine learning that explains why techniques like ensembles and regularization reduce error, turning a supposed tradeoff into something you can win.
